sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 13-year-old's father, presents with right elbow pain that started approximately one week ago while playing lacrosse with his children.
He describes the pain as sharp and radiating down the forearm, exacerbated by movement.
He reports that his son got rough with him and was checking him several times with a lacrosse stick, which may have caused the injury.
The patient has tried taking Advil, which provided some relief.

Past medical history: The patient has a past medical history of hypertension, for which he is currently taking 20 mg of lisinopril daily, and diabetes, for which he is taking 500 mg of metformin daily.
He reports following a Mediterranean diet and avoiding added salt.
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications:
Lisinopril 20 mg daily for hypertension
Metformin 500 mg daily for diabetes
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure was 150/90, which is considered high.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs were clear bilaterally, and his heart exam showed a grade 2 out of 6 systolic ejection murmur, which is a pre-existing condition.
The elbow exam showed pain to palpation of the olecranon area of the posterior elbow, mild pain with flexion and extension, and normal range of motion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a right elbow contusion likely caused by his son's lacrosse stick, resulting in pain and swelling.
Problem list:
Elbow contusion; Hypertension; Diabetes; Systolic ejection murmur; Past medical history of hypertension; Past medical history of diabetes
Plan:
I prescribed ibuprofen 800 mg twice daily for pain and swelling, and instructed the patient to ice the elbow three times a day for 20 minutes to help with swelling and pain.
I advised him to avoid playing lacrosse with his son for the next couple of weeks to allow the injury to heal.
The patient's hypertension and diabetes management will remain unchanged, with continued lisinopril 20 mg daily and metformin 500 mg daily.
We will obtain an A1c to monitor his diabetes control.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again to review the results of the A1c and to adjust his metformin dosage as necessary.
